children.name: Lacerta
children.description: True lizards of open habitats. The Sand Lizard is one of only two lizard species breeding in Sweden — males develop vivid green flanks in breeding season, transforming from drab brown to brilliant green over several weeks in spring. It requires loose sandy soil for egg-laying and is declining across its northern range.

children.name: Lacerta agilis
children.description: One of two lizard species native to Sweden; breeding males develop vivid green flanks; requires loose sandy soil for egg-laying; declining in northern range; Swedish populations are protected.

children.name: Lacerta viridis
children.description: Brilliant iridescent green; males have a blue throat in breeding season; largest lacertid in Europe at up to 40 cm.

children.name: Lacerta bilineata
children.description: Recently split from L. viridis; France and Iberia.

children.name: Zootoca vivipara
children.description: Sweden's most widespread reptile; found from southern Sweden to the Arctic fell; viviparous in most populations — gives birth to live young rather than laying eggs; a critical adaptation to cold northern climates.

children.name: Podarcis muralis
children.description: Extreme generalist; colonising northward in a warming climate; now established in UK, Belgium, Netherlands; the fastest-diversifying lizard in Europe.

children.name: Podarcis siculus
children.description: Classic study system in rapid parallel evolution following island introductions.

children.name: Timon lepidus
children.description: Europe's largest lizard at up to 90 cm; brilliant blue ocelli along flanks; Iberian endemic; IUCN Near Threatened.
